questions.question_text: Which Montana law governs condominium ownership and creation?
questions.explanation: The Montana Unit Ownership Act governs condominium ownership, including creation requirements, unit owner rights, common elements, and HOA governance. Creation requires filing a declaration, bylaws, and plat.

questions.question_text: A developer plans to create a condominium project in Missoula. Which documents must be filed to establish the project?
questions.explanation: Under the Montana Unit Ownership Act, a condominium is created by recording a declaration (MCA 70-23-305) together with certified floor plans showing the units and common areas (70-23-306) and the association bylaws (70-23-307) — all recorded simultaneously with the county clerk and recorder. The county must approve the declaration before recording (70-23-304).

questions.question_text: A condo owner in Helena asks what she actually owns. What is the correct description of her ownership?
questions.correct_answer: Her unit plus a share of common elements
distractors: Her unit only, not common areas
Shares of stock in the association
questions.explanation: Under the Montana Unit Ownership Act, each unit owner owns their individual unit in fee simple plus an undivided share of the common elements (hallways, lobbies, grounds, etc.). Common elements are owned collectively by all unit owners.

questions.question_text: A Montana HOA levies a special assessment that an owner refuses to pay. What enforcement tool does the HOA have?
questions.correct_answer: File an assessment lien on the unit
distractors: Report the owner to the state agency
questions.explanation: Montana HOAs can place an assessment lien on a unit for unpaid assessments. These liens may take priority over certain other encumbrances. The HOA can ultimately foreclose on the lien to collect the debt.

questions.question_text: Are CC&Rs recorded by a Montana HOA binding on future purchasers of units in the development?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, they run with the land
distractors: No, only if the buyer signs them
questions.explanation: CC&Rs (Covenants, Conditions, and Restrictions) are recorded and run with the land, binding all current and future owners. Purchasing a unit in the development means accepting the CC&Rs as a condition of ownership.

questions.question_text: Which document is NOT typically part of a Montana condo resale package?
questions.explanation: Montana statute requires a condo seller, on request, to furnish the Unit Ownership Act, the association bylaws, and its administrative regulations before a buy-sell agreement is signed, with a 72-hour delay during which the buyer may withdraw (MCA 70-23-613). In practice, resale packages also customarily include HOA financial statements, reserve information, and pending-assessment and litigation disclosures. The seller's personal mortgage statement is private financing information and is never part of the association's package.

questions.question_text: A prospective condo buyer in Bozeman learns the HOA has no reserve fund. Why is this information significant?
distractors: The HOA is operating illegally now
The building must be condemned soon
questions.explanation: Without a reserve fund, the HOA lacks savings for major repairs and capital improvements. This means unit owners will likely face special assessments for unexpected expenses. Reserve fund status is an important disclosure item for prospective buyers.

questions.question_text: What document summarizes a Montana condo unit's financial obligations and association status for a prospective buyer?
questions.explanation: In common interest resales, the document that consolidates a unit's assessment obligations, association finances, and pending matters is known as a resale certificate. Montana's statute itself is narrower — MCA 70-23-613 requires only the Unit Ownership Act, bylaws, and association regulations, with a 72-hour withdrawal window — so the fuller resale certificate is produced by custom and contract rather than statutory mandate.

questions.question_text: A Montana condo association votes to install a new elevator in the building. The cost will be $200,000. How is this typically funded?
questions.correct_answer: Special assessment to all unit owners
distractors: The developer covers all the costs
A state grant program covers it
questions.explanation: Major capital improvements in a Montana condo are typically funded through special assessments allocated to all unit owners based on their ownership percentage. If the reserve fund is insufficient, the association levies a special assessment.

questions.question_text: A Montana condo association is being sued by a former contractor. Must this be disclosed to prospective buyers?
distractors: No, only previously settled cases matter
Only if the HOA lost the lawsuit
Only if the buyer asks about lawsuits
questions.explanation: Yes. Pending litigation against the association can produce judgments and special assessments, making it an adverse material fact. Montana licensees must disclose adverse material facts concerning the property to buyers (MCA 37-51-313), and sellers must disclose known adverse material facts — including legal issues — before or with the contract (MCA 70-20-502). Disclosure is required whether or not the buyer asks.

questions.question_text: In a Montana condominium, who collectively owns the common elements such as hallways and parking areas?
questions.correct_answer: All unit owners as a group
distractors: The HOA as a separate entity
questions.explanation: Under the Unit Ownership Act, the common elements are owned by all unit owners together, each holding an undivided percentage interest set in the declaration based on the unit's value relative to all units (MCA 70-23-403). That interest stays permanently attached to the unit and cannot be partitioned (70-23-404, -405).

questions.question_text: A buyer's agent reviewing condo documents discovers the HOA plans a $5,000 per-unit special assessment next quarter. What should the agent do?
questions.correct_answer: Disclose this to the buyer immediately
distractors: Wait until after closing to mention it
Advise the buyer to skip the review
Contact the HOA to delay the vote
questions.explanation: Upcoming special assessments are material facts that must be disclosed to prospective buyers. The agent has a duty to inform the buyer of this significant financial obligation, which will affect the total cost of ownership.

questions.question_text: Which statement about Montana HOA assessment liens is correct?
questions.correct_answer: They may take priority over some liens
distractors: They are always subordinate to all liens
They expire after one year automatically
questions.explanation: Montana HOA assessment liens may take priority over certain other encumbrances on the property. This gives the association a powerful collection tool, as the lien can be foreclosed if the owner fails to pay.

questions.question_text: A Montana condo buyer receives the HOA's annual budget showing a 15% increase in monthly dues. This information comes from which disclosure item?
questions.explanation: HOA financial statements, including budgets and fee schedules, are standard items in a condo resale package and are material information an agent must not conceal. Montana's statutory minimum (MCA 70-23-613) covers the governing documents, so buyers should specifically request and review the financials — a significant dues increase directly affects the cost of ownership.

questions.question_text: What is the primary purpose of the bylaws in a Montana condominium association?
questions.correct_answer: Govern the operation of the association
distractors: Describe the physical boundaries of units
Set the property tax rates for units
questions.explanation: Bylaws establish the rules for governing the condominium association, including meeting procedures, voting rights, board elections, and officer duties. The declaration describes physical boundaries, while bylaws address organizational governance.
